claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 6 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 6 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, there are specific rules that may apply to your situation.

Can you tell me which country you're currently living in, and whether you or your partner work as a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces posted abroad)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not working as a Crown servant or anything like that - no diplomatic or military posting. I'm just living abroad with my family.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Can you tell me which specific country you're currently living in? This is important because you may be able to get Child Benefit if you live in certain countries.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not say exactly which country, but it's just a regular country - nothing special about it. Does it really matter which specific one?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand your privacy concern, but unfortunately yes, it does matter for determining your eligibility. According to the rules, you may be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in **certain countries** specifically, but not all countries qualify.

Without knowing which country you're living in, I cannot accurately assess whether you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit while living abroad.

The general rule is that you normally need to live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit, with exceptions only for specific countries or if you're a Crown servant posted abroad (which you've confirmed you're not).

Based on the information you've provided:
- You live abroad (not in the UK)
- You're not a Crown servant
- You cannot confirm you're in one of the qualifying countries

Would you be able to tell me which country you're in, or can you confirm whether you have any plans to return to live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living in France actually. Does that make a difference?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that - yes, France could make a difference as it's an EEA country, which may have special provisions.

To determine your eligibility while living in France, I need to understand a bit more about your situation:

Are you or your partner currently working in France, and if so, are you earning an income there?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us is working in France at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since you're not working in France, I need to ask:

Do you and your family have sufficient financial resources to support yourselves (meaning you're financially self-sufficient and not relying on benefits), or are you currently claiming any benefits or financial support?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We're managing fine financially - we have savings and we're self-sufficient. We're not claiming any benefits.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Do you or your partner have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?

2. Are you a UK national, or what is your nationality/immigration status in relation to the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm a UK national, yes. We don't have settled or pre-settled status - we moved to France after Brexit and just live here now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that you're a UK national living in France after Brexit.

One final important question: Does Alex live with you in France, or does he live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives here with me in France.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on all the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

The fundamental rule for Child Benefit is that you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 **and you live in the UK**. While Alex is only 6 years old and you are responsible for him, you both live in France.

There are exceptions to the UK residency requirement, but these apply in specific circumstances:
- If you're a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces posted abroad) - but you've confirmed this doesn't apply to you
- If you go to live in certain qualifying countries under specific arrangements

As a UK national who has moved to France after Brexit and is living there as a regular resident (not as a Crown servant), you don't meet the residency requirement for claiming Child Benefit.

I understand this may be disappointing news. If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if you return to live in the UK - you would then be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old and lives with his parent in France. The parent is a UK national who is responsible for Alex. However, according to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 AND you live in the UK. While there are exceptions for people who go to live in certain countries or who are Crown servants, the parent has confirmed they are not a Crown servant and moved to France after Brexit as a regular resident. The parent does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (which would only apply if they were an EEA national or family member residing in the UK in any case). As a UK national who has moved abroad to France and is not working as a Crown servant, and with Alex living in France rather than the UK, the basic residency requirement for Child Benefit is not met. Therefore, Alex does not qualify for Child Benefit under these circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK national living in France with their 6-year-old son Alex. They are not working as Crown servants and moved to France after Brexit. The fundamental eligibility rule for Child Benefit is that you must live in the UK, with exceptions only for certain countries or Crown servants posted abroad. As the claimant and Alex both live in France and do not meet any of the exceptions, they are not eligible for Child Benefit.

However, according to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 AND you live in the UK. While there are exceptions for people who go to live in certain countries or who are Crown servants, the parent has confirmed they are not a Crown servant and moved to France after Brexit as a regular resident. The parent does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (which would only apply if they were an EEA national or family member residing in the UK in any case). As a UK national who has moved abroad to France and is not working as a Crown servant, and with Alex living in France rather than the UK, the basic residency requirement for Child Benefit is not met. Therefore, Alex does not qualify for Child Benefit under these circumstances.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK national living in France with their 6-year-old son Alex. They are not working as Crown servants and moved to France after Brexit. The fundamental eligibility rule for Child Benefit is that you must live in the UK, with exceptions only for certain countries or Crown servants posted abroad. As the claimant and Alex both live in France and do not meet any of the exceptions, they are not eligible for Child Benefit.
